topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into an incomprehensible mess. *Effective immediately, the institution will cease operations.* My hands started to tremble so violently I had to grip the desk edge to steady them. Every sound in the dorm room—the distant hum of the refrigerator, the ticking of the clock—seemed amplified, sharp and intrusive. I reread the message, searching for a typo, a mistake, anything that could make it untrue. But the official seal was there, the language was cold and final. A sudden, hot flush spread across my neck, and I pushed back from the chair, knocking a stack of textbooks to the floor with a loud clatter. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I began pacing the small confines of my room, my gaze darting frantically between the door and the window, searching for an escape route that simply wasn't there.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the meeting in my head, the hushed, grave tones of the dean, the way his eyes had slid away just before he delivered the news. Now, the reality was crashing down, heavy and suffocating. I stood by the window, looking out at the manicured quad, and felt a strange, dizzying emptiness in my chest. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic drum solo demanding attention. I tried to take a deep, steadying breath, but it caught in my throat, turning into a ragged gasp. I started pacing the length of my room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I kept touching my face, feeling the clammy sweat prickle on my skin. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. "This can't be happening." The silence of the empty building seemed to mock my frantic internal state.

[story 3]

The notification flashed on my phone, and the color seemed to drain from the room. I dropped the device onto the worn carpet, the sound swallowed by the sudden, overwhelming silence. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges. I couldn't seem to focus on anything; the familiar posters on the wall seemed to writhe before my eyes. A wave of sheer disorientation washed over me, making the floor feel unsteady beneath my feet. I stumbled toward the door, my legs feeling disconnected from my body. I fumbled for the doorknob, my fingers clumsy and unresponsive. A desperate need to bolt, to run toward anything that wasn't this suffocating enclosure, seized me. I slammed my fist against the wood, a dull thud that barely registered over the roaring in my ears.
